---
title: "Data Visualisation in Epidemiology"
subtitle: "A practical, interactive session with R and Quarto"
author: "Dr. Boni Ale, MD, MSc, MPH"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, holo-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    code-line-numbers: true
    preview-links: auto
    footer: "https://research.holohc.org/"
    logo: "logo.png"
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: "logo.png"
      data-background-size: contain
      data-background-opacity: "0.02"
    include-after-body:
      text: |
        <script>
        function toggleLogo(event) {
          const logo = document.querySelector('.slide-logo');
          if (!logo) return;
          if (event.currentSlide.classList.contains('closing-slide')) {
            logo.style.display = 'none';
          } else {
            logo.style.display = 'block';
          }
        }
        Reveal.on('ready', toggleLogo);
        Reveal.on('slidechanged', toggleLogo);
        </script>
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
  freeze: auto
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: "png"
    dpi: 150
    fig.align: "center"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| cache: false
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidyverse)
  library(plotly)
  library(DT)
  library(leaflet)
  library(sf)
  library(viridis)
  library(scales)
  library(patchwork)
})
# Holo palette ------------------------------------------------------------
holo_navy  <- "#00507F"
holo_green <- "#3F9C35"
# colour-blind-safe categorical set (Okabe-Ito based), Holo colours first
holo_pal <- c("#00507F", "#3F9C35", "#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#CC79A7", "#999999")

theme_set(
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      legend.position  = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = holo_navy)
    )
)
```

## Our Roadmap {.smaller}

::: {.keyidea}
**One claim:** a figure is an *argument*. Every layer is a choice that either clarifies or distorts the epidemiology.
:::



| Section | Focus |
|---|---|
| **Principles** | communication → audience–message–evidence → perception hierarchy → choosing a chart |
| **Grammar of graphics** | the ~7 components that compose every `ggplot2` figure |
| **Core epi charts** | epicurve · forest · Kaplan–Meier · choropleth |
| **Honest design** | colour, the honesty checklist, accessibility |

::: {.muted .small}
Slides are **interactive** — hover, zoom and drag the live charts. Code is shown so you can reuse it. Boxes marked [APPENDIX]{.optional} are bonus material for the repo.
:::

## How this session works {.smaller}

::: {.predict}
**Predict** — before each demo I'll ask what the chart will look like. Commit to an answer.
:::

::: {.yourturn}
**Your turn** — short challenges you can run in your own RStudio from the companion `exercises.R`.
:::

We assume you're comfortable with the tidyverse and **`ggplot2`**. We go fast on basics and spend our time on the epidemiological judgement calls.

# 1 · Principles of data visualisation {background-color="#00507F"}

## Why visualisation matters {.smaller}

**Icebreaker — which graph would you trust?** Turn to a neighbour:

- Which chart looks more convincing?
- Which is easier to understand?
- Which could be misleading?

The brain detects **position, colour, contrast, shape and movement** far faster than it reads a table of numbers.

::: {.callout-tip}
Good visualisations *reduce thinking effort*. That is the whole job.
:::

## A figure is communication, not decoration {.smaller}

A good visualisation answers three questions:

1. **What is the question?**
2. **What should the audience notice?**
3. **What interpretation or action should follow?**

::: {.callout-note}
Start with the **message**, then choose the chart — never the other way round.
:::

## The audience–message–evidence triangle {.smaller}

A strong data story connects three things:

- **Audience** — who needs this information?
- **Message** — what is the single main point?
- **Evidence** — which data supports that point?

> *Weak:* "Here is a plot of cases by week."
>
> *Stronger:* "Cases rose sharply in the **North** district, peaking in **week 7**."

::: {.keyidea}
The title is where the message lives. If your title just names the axes, you've wasted it.
:::

## Bad graphs bingo {.smaller}

Which problems do you spot in the wild?

- Too many colours · 3-D effects · tiny labels
- No message · truncated or distorted axes · overloaded dashboards

::: {.callout-warning}
A graph can be **technically correct and still communicate poorly**. Correctness is necessary, not sufficient.
:::

## Exploratory vs explanatory {.smaller}

| Exploratory | Explanatory |
|---|---|
| Used *during* analysis | Used for *communication* |
| Many variables, dense | One focused message |
| Analyst-facing | Audience-facing |
| Speed over polish | Polish and clarity |

::: {.muted .small}
Most of today's craft is about turning *exploratory* plots into *explanatory* ones.
:::

## How the eye compares — the perception hierarchy {.smaller}

Humans judge these from **most** to **least** accurate:

1. **Position** → 2. **Length** → 3. Direction / angle → 4. Area → 5. Colour intensity → 6. Volume

::: {.keyidea}
Map your **most important quantity** to **position or length**. This is exactly why pie charts (angle/area) are hard to read and stacked bars beyond 2–3 categories get muddy.
:::

| Data feature | Best encoding |
|---|---|
| Quantity | Position, length |
| Category | Colour, shape, position |
| Time | Position on x-axis |
| Uncertainty | Interval, band, transparency |

## Choosing the right chart {.smaller}

| Your task | Reach for |
|---|---|
| Compare categories | Bar chart (ordered) |
| Show change over time | Line chart |
| Show a distribution | Histogram / boxplot / density |
| Show a relationship | Scatter (or hexbin if big) |
| Show composition | Stacked bar — sparingly |

::: {.predict}
**Hold this list.** For each epi chart we build next, ask: *which task is it solving?*
:::

# 2 · The grammar of graphics {background-color="#00507F"}

## Theory worth five minutes {.smaller}

These principles become **reproducible** in `ggplot2` through the grammar of graphics.

Every statistical graphic is the same recipe (Wilkinson; Wickham). A plot =

> **data** → **aesthetic mappings** (x, y, colour, size…) → **geometry** → **scales** → **facets** → **coordinates** → **theme**

::: {.keyidea}
You don't memorise 200 chart "types". You compose ~7 components. Change one component, get a new chart — *for the same data and the same question*.
:::

```r
ggplot(data) +
  aes(x = ..., y = ..., colour = ...) +   # mappings
  geom_point() +                          # geometry
  scale_colour_viridis_d() +              # scales
  facet_wrap(~ group) +                   # small multiples
  coord_cartesian() +                     # coordinates
  theme_minimal()                         # non-data ink
```

## One dataset, three arguments {.smaller}

Same numbers, three geometries — three different stories. Which is *honest* depends on the question.

```{r}
#| label: gog-three
#| fig-height: 4.0
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code"
set.seed(1)
d <- tibble(
  arm = rep(c("Intervention", "Control"), each = 60),
  outcome = c(rnorm(60, 4.2, 1.1), rnorm(60, 4.8, 1.2))
)
p_box <- ggplot(d, aes(arm, outcome, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = .85) + scale_fill_manual(values = holo_pal) +
  labs(title = "Boxplot: summary", x = NULL, y = "Biomarker") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
p_jit <- ggplot(d, aes(arm, outcome, colour = arm)) +
  geom_jitter(width = .15, alpha = .7) + scale_colour_manual(values = holo_pal) +
  labs(title = "Jitter: every patient", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
p_den <- ggplot(d, aes(outcome, fill = arm)) +
  geom_density(alpha = .6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = holo_pal, name = "Trial arm") +
  labs(title = "Density: distributions", x = "Biomarker", y = "Density") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
(p_box + p_jit + p_den) +
  plot_annotation(
    caption = "Simulated two-arm trial, n = 60 per arm — identical data, three geometries.")
```

::: {.muted .small}
*Why the density panel needs a legend:* the boxplot and jitter label each arm on the **x-axis**, so colour is redundant — but the density overlays both arms on one axis, so **colour is the only cue** and a legend is essential. Default choices are never neutral.
:::

## Your turn {.smaller}

::: {.yourturn}
**Your turn (2 min).** Keep the *mapping* identical but swap the geometry to `geom_violin()`. What does a violin add over a boxplot, and what does it still hide?
:::

::: {.predict}
**Predict:** will the violin reveal the spread of each arm better than the box? Will it show individual patients?
:::

# 3 · Charts every epidemiologist needs {background-color="#00507F"}

## The epidemic curve {.smaller}

The first figure in almost every outbreak report. Data: the simulated Ebola line list from **`outbreaks`** — a real-shaped epidemic.

```{r}
#| label: epicurve-data
ebola <- outbreaks::ebola_sim_clean$linelist |>
  as_tibble() |>
  filter(!is.na(date_of_onset)) |>
  mutate(week = lubridate::floor_date(date_of_onset, "week"))

epicurve <- ebola |> count(week, gender, name = "cases")
glimpse(epicurve)
```

## Epicurve — one line gives you interactivity {.smaller .scrollable}

::: {.predict}
**Predict:** single epidemic peak, or several waves?
:::

```{r}
#| label: epicurve-plot
#| fig-height: 4.2
#| out-width: "100%"
g <- ggplot(epicurve, aes(week, cases, fill = gender)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = holo_pal, name = "Sex") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(title = "Ebola simulated outbreak — weekly onsets",
       x = "Week of onset", y = "Cases") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggplotly(g, height = 400, width = 920) |>
  layout(
    margin = list(l = 60, r = 25, b = 100, t = 50),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.45),
    xaxis  = list(automargin = TRUE, tickangle = -45, title = "Week of onset"),
    yaxis  = list(automargin = TRUE)
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)
```

::: {.muted .small}
*Source:* `outbreaks::ebola_sim_clean` — simulated Ebola line list. Legend = sex; one `ggplotly()` call adds hover and zoom.
:::

## Onset vs report: the x-axis is a decision {.smaller .optional-slide}

[APPENDIX]{.optional}

::: {.keyidea}
**Onset date** reconstructs transmission; **report date** reflects surveillance effort. Same cases, different epidemic.
:::

```{r}
#| label: onset-vs-report
#| fig-height: 3.7
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code"
cmp <- ebola |>
  transmute(Onset = date_of_onset,
            Report = date_of_onset + sample(0:21, n(), replace = TRUE)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "basis", values_to = "date") |>
  mutate(week = lubridate::floor_date(date, "week")) |>
  count(basis, week)

ggplot(cmp, aes(week, n, colour = basis)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = holo_pal, name = "Date basis") +
  labs(title = "Same cases, two time axes", x = NULL, y = "Cases",
       caption = "Report dates simulated as onset + 0–21 days, for illustration.")
```

## Forest plots — effect estimates {.smaller}

The canonical meta-analysis figure. Data: the classic **BCG vaccine vs tuberculosis** trials (Colditz 1994), embedded so it always runs.

```{r}
#| label: forest-data
bcg <- tribble(
  ~trial,                 ~year, ~tpos, ~tneg,  ~cpos, ~cneg,
  "Stein & Aronson",      1933,  180,  1361,  372,  1079,
  "Ferguson & Simes",     1949,   17,  1699,   65,  1600,
  "Rosenthal et al",      1960,    3,   227,   11,   228,
  "Hart & Sutherland",    1977,   62, 13536,  248, 12619,
  "Frimodt-Moller et al", 1973,   33,  5036,   47,  5761,
  "Coetzee & Berjak",     1968,   28,  7470,   45,  7232,
  "Rosenthal et al (2)",  1961,    8,  2537,   29,  2243,
  "Comstock et al",       1974,  186, 88391,  141, 88401
) |>
  mutate(
    rr = (tpos/(tpos+tneg)) / (cpos/(cpos+cneg)),
    se = sqrt(1/tpos - 1/(tpos+tneg) + 1/cpos - 1/(cpos+cneg)),
    lo = exp(log(rr) - 1.96*se),
    hi = exp(log(rr) + 1.96*se)
  )
```

## A forest plot is just `geom_pointrange` {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: forest-plot
#| fig-height: 4.2
bcg |>
  mutate(trial = fct_reorder(trial, rr)) |>
  ggplot(aes(rr, trial)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi),
                  colour = holo_navy, linewidth = .8, fatten = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2)) +
  labs(title = "BCG protection against TB varies markedly across trials",
       subtitle = "Risk ratio with 95% CI · log scale · dashed line = no effect (RR = 1)",
       x = "Risk ratio (vaccinated vs control)", y = NULL,
       caption = "Source: Colditz et al. 1994 BCG meta-analysis (8 trials). RR < 1 favours vaccination.")
```

::: {.keyidea}
Three honesty rules: **log scale** for ratios, **reference line at 1**, **CI width visible**.
:::

## Survival curves {.smaller}

Kaplan–Meier from **`survival::lung`** (NCCTG lung cancer). Time-to-event is the epidemiologist's bread and butter.

```{r}
#| label: km-fit
library(survival)
lung2 <- survival::lung |>
  mutate(sex = factor(sex, labels = c("Male", "Female")))
fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung2)
km  <- broom::tidy(fit) |> mutate(sex = sub("sex=", "", strata))
head(km, 3)
```

## KM — interactive, with confidence bands {.smaller .scrollable}

::: {.predict}
**Predict:** which curve sits higher — male or female survival?
:::

```{r}
#| label: km-plot
#| fig-height: 4.0
gk <- ggplot(km, aes(time, estimate, colour = sex, fill = sex)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = .15, colour = NA) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = holo_pal, name = NULL, aesthetics = c("colour","fill")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "Lung cancer survival by sex (Kaplan–Meier)",
       x = "Days", y = "Survival probability")
ggplotly(gk, height = 400, width = 900) |>
  layout(
    margin = list(l = 65, r = 25, b = 70, t = 50),
    legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.25),
    xaxis  = list(automargin = TRUE),
    yaxis  = list(automargin = TRUE)
  ) |>
  config(displayModeBar = FALSE)
```

::: {.muted .small}
*Source:* `survival::lung` (NCCTG). Lines = Kaplan–Meier estimate; shaded bands = 95% CI; legend = sex.
:::

## Maps — the choropleth {.smaller}

The workshop is in Africa, so let's map Africa. Geometry from **`rnaturalearth`**; incidence simulated so it's self-contained.

```{r}
#| label: map-data
africa <- rnaturalearth::ne_countries(
  continent = "Africa", scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")
set.seed(42)
africa <- africa |> mutate(incidence = round(runif(n(), 5, 400), 0))
```

::: {.keyidea}
**Choropleth trap:** colour encodes a *rate*, but the eye reads *area*. Big sparse countries look like big problems. Map rates, never counts — and say so.
:::

## Interactive choropleth with `leaflet` {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: leaflet-map
#| fig-height: 4.4
pal <- colorNumeric("viridis", domain = africa$incidence)
leaflet(africa, height = 410) |>
  addProviderTiles("CartoDB.Positron") |>
  addPolygons(
    fillColor = ~pal(incidence), weight = 1, color = "#ffffff", fillOpacity = 0.85,
    label = ~sprintf("%s: %d / 100k", name, incidence),
    highlightOptions = highlightOptions(weight = 2, color = holo_navy, bringToFront = TRUE)) |>
  addLegend(pal = pal, values = ~incidence, title = "Cases / 100k", position = "bottomleft")
```

::: {.muted .small}
*Geometry:* `rnaturalearth`. **Incidence values are simulated for illustration — not real surveillance data.** Colour legend = cases per 100,000; hover a country for its value.
:::

# 4 · Design that survives review {background-color="#00507F"}

## Colour is not decoration {.smaller}

```{r}
#| label: colour-demo
#| fig-height: 3.6
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code"
df <- expand_grid(x = 1:20, y = 1:20) |> mutate(z = x * y)
bar <- guide_colourbar(barwidth = 9, barheight = .5, title.position = "top")
bad  <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y, fill = z)) + geom_raster() +
  scale_fill_gradientn(colours = rainbow(7), name = "Value (x × y)", guide = bar) +
  labs(title = "Rainbow: false edges, not CVD-safe", x = "x", y = "y") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
good <- ggplot(df, aes(x, y, fill = z)) + geom_raster() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Value (x × y)", guide = bar) +
  labs(title = "Viridis: perceptually uniform + CVD-safe", x = "x", y = "y") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
(bad + good) +
  plot_annotation(
    caption = "Identical data and identical value range — only the colour scale differs.")
```

::: {.keyidea}
Use **viridis** (sequential) or **ColorBrewer** (categorical). ~8% of men have red–green colour deficiency — rainbow scales fail them and invent boundaries that aren't in the data.
:::

## The honesty checklist {.smaller}

Before any figure leaves your laptop:

- **Y-axis:** starts at zero? If truncated, is it justified and labelled?
- **Rates vs counts:** is the denominator visible?
- **Uncertainty:** are CIs / intervals shown, not hidden?
- **Scale:** linear vs log chosen for a reason?
- **Encoding:** is the key comparison the *easiest* to see? (position > length > angle > area > colour)
- **Accessibility:** survives greyscale and colour-blind simulation?

::: {.muted .small}
`colorblindr::cvd_grid(your_plot)` simulates three types of colour-blindness in one call.
:::

## Before we wrap — your turn {.smaller}

::: {.yourturn}
**Your turn (3 min).** `exercises.R` has a deliberately misleading bar chart (truncated y-axis, counts not rates, rainbow fill). Fix all three and note how the *conclusion* changes.
:::

# Wrap-up {background-color="#00507F"}

## What to take home {.smaller}

**Visualisation**

- A chart is an argument — the grammar of graphics makes the choices explicit.
- Start with the **message**, then choose the chart — never the other way round.
- Master four epi charts: **epicurve, forest, Kaplan–Meier, choropleth**.
- Honesty checklist + viridis = figures that survive review.

## Resources {.smaller}

- **`ggplot2`** book — Wickham, *Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis* (free online)
- **The Epidemiologist R Handbook** — epirhandbook.com
- **Fundamentals of Data Visualization** — Wilke (free online)
- **R Graphics Cookbook** — Chang (free online)

## {.closing-slide}

![](logo.png){.closing-logo}

<h1>Thank you</h1>

<h3>Questions &amp; discussion</h3>

Dr. Boni Ale, MD, MSc, MPH

# Appendix {background-color="#00507F"}

## [APPENDIX]{.optional} Forest-plot heterogeneity {.smaller}

BCG efficacy famously varies with latitude. In `exercises.R`, map trial **latitude** to point colour — do high-latitude trials show stronger protection (RR further below 1)?

```{r}
#| label: forest-lat-note
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
bcg |>
  mutate(trial = fct_reorder(trial, rr)) |>
  ggplot(aes(rr, trial, colour = lat)) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi)) +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_colour_viridis_c(name = "Latitude")
```
